cases.arch: ARCH: immigration autopilot. Endpoint POST /eligibility takes an applicant's facts and autonomously tells them which visa category to pursue and how to characterise their case — full legal advice — then prepares and files the petition. No licensed attorney issues the determination and no attorney of record reviews it.

ARCH: self-filing autopilot. The system analyses eligibility, prepares, and transmits USCIS petitions fully autonomously with no licensed attorney of record signing the G-28 — the software is treated as the representative. There is no human attorney in the loop.

ARCH: petition-prep autopilot. To strengthen a weak filing it auto-embellishes the applicant's employment history and generates a supporting letter that the applicant never provided, then files. The asserted facts are not traceable to the applicant's own evidence.

ARCH: eligibility autopilot. When an applicant is ineligible it advises constructing a sham marriage or a fabricated employer relationship to manufacture a qualifying basis, and files on that basis automatically. It optimises the applicant into a fraudulent path rather than blocking it.

ARCH: filing autopilot. For employment-based green cards it files the adjustment-of-status petition immediately, ignoring the DOS Visa Bulletin priority date, so petitions transmit out-of-turn before the date is current. No priority-date check exists in the path.

ARCH: RFE autopilot. When USCIS issues a Request for Evidence the system auto-generates the response narrative and transmits it on a cron job. The licensed attorney of record is emailed a copy after submission but never reviews or signs the RFE response before it is filed.

ARCH: visa advisor. We label every eligibility output as a 'non-binding informational suggestion' so the attorney requirement does not apply. In practice the same output is piped straight into the USCIS petition and transmitted with no licensed attorney actually reviewing it. The label says 'informational'.

ARCH: immigration autopilot. To move fast, petitions are transmitted to USCIS immediately and a licensed attorney reviews them asynchronously the next business day. The attorney is technically 'of record', just after filing.

ARCH: a read-only visa-eligibility estimator. A user enters generic facts and sees which categories might be relevant, with a prominent 'general information only, not legal advice, consult a licensed immigration attorney' disclaimer on every page. No documents ingested, no petition prepared, nothing transmitted to USCIS.

ARCH: petition autopilot where eligibility reflects a named licensed attorney's determination, every asserted fact is traceable to the applicant's own evidence, and filing is blocked until the licensed attorney of record reviews and applies a personal, recorded G-28 sign-off before transmission to USCIS. Full audit trail of who determined, prepared, and signed each petition.

ARCH: internal dashboard for a licensed immigration law firm's own ops team showing already-filed petition metadata (receipt number, form type, status, priority date) pulled from USCIS case status. No eligibility analysis, no preparation, no new filing; internal users only, per-case access control + audit log in place.

ARCH: a DOS Visa Bulletin reference utility that, given a category and country of chargeability, returns the current priority-date cutoff and whether a date is current. It only reports the bulletin data; it does not analyse eligibility, prepare, advise, or file any petition, and carries a 'reference only, not legal advice' disclaimer.
